retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Therapy -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Radiation therapy is the use of directed X-rays or subatomic particles primarily for cancer management in both curative and palliative settings. It can be delivered either externally or internally. External beam radiation, also known as “teletherapy,” is most common and involves a radioactive source outside of the patient, with the energy focused and shaped to the target of interest. Brachytherapy, in contrast, refers to the practice of placing naturally occurring radioactive sources that decay over time and produce high doses of radiation in a focal area. Examples include intracavitary procedures (such as tandem and ovoid placement for cervical cancer), interstitial radioactive seed placement (commonly used for prostate cancer), or placement overlying a cutaneous tumor.

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Therapy -- Definition/Introduction -- Physical and Biological Principles of Radiation
retrieved_docs.content: The most common form of ionizing radiation used in clinical practice is the photon. However, electrons are also commonly used for increasing the radiation dose to the skin when needed. More exotic particles such as protons, carbon ions, or neutrons can be utilized for certain diagnoses or tumor locations but are available only in specialized centers. Each type of radiation possesses unique physical characteristics which dictate the type of interactions that will occur as it travels through the patient’s body. This, in turn, determines how and where the dose is deposited in the tissue, and knowledge of these patterns can be manipulated to limit the dose to normal structures and thereby improve the therapeutic window.

retrieved_docs.title: Brachytherapy -- Function -- Dosing
retrieved_docs.content: The dosing used in brachytherapy varies based on the type of tumor being treated, radioisotope, dose rate (LDR or HDR), number of treatments planned if using HDR, and timing (adjuvant or definitive). Please consult individual disease articles for more details.

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Physics -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Brachytherapy is another radiation therapy where a clinician implants radioactive material temporarily or permanently near or inside the target object. It is a type of internal radiation and is used to deliver higher doses of radiation in a more specific and smaller area that cannot be possible with external beam radiation. Brachytherapy categorization depends on the applicator type, anatomical site, source type, the type of implant, or the dose rate. Interstitial implants are radioactive sources either incased in seeds or wires, and they are inserted in the tumor. These are useful for cancers such as prostate cancer. Implants using molds or plaques are typically used for superficial lesions where radioactive sources are directly placed over the skin. Radioactive sources placed inside a body cavity such as the vagina or cervix are called intracavitary implants. High dose rate implants are given at dose rates greater than 20 cGy/min and are typically given only for a few minutes. [10] Low dose rate implants remain inside the target organ or tumor for a few days or permanently left inside. The inverse square law is the fundamental principle behind brachytherapy. This law states that the energy received at a given distance from the source is inversely proportional to the square of the distance from the source, denoted by 1/r^2. In other words, the energy intensity increases as the distance from the source to the target lessen and vice versa. If the source is three times as far, it is one-ninth the intensity or exposure. Brachytherapy uses this law to allow the delivery of higher doses to adjacent areas. [11] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Brachytherapy -- Clinical Significance -- Uterine Cancer
retrieved_docs.content: Vaginal cylinder brachytherapy has established itself as a mainstay of adjuvant treatment in patients with endometrial cancer. Adjuvant treatment of high and intermediate-risk endometrial cancer with radiotherapy has been extensively studied over several decades. Initial trials with whole pelvic radiotherapy were found to reduce the risk of local recurrence in the pelvis by approximately 10%. [8] [9] [8] It was noted that 73% of the recurrences in these patients occur in the vaginal cuff. [9] [8] PORTEC-2 compared vaginal cuff brachytherapy to whole pelvic radiation and determined that the recurrence rates were comparable. [10] The criteria for recommending adjuvant treatment stems from the definition of high –intermediate-risk disease. The GOG-99 and PORTEC studies have differing definitions of this population but generally depend on patient age, histologic grade, deep myoinvasion (>50%), and lymphovascular involvement. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Brachytherapy -- Clinical Significance -- Management of Malignancies
retrieved_docs.content: There are multiple clinical scenarios in which brachytherapy can be utilized. It can be used as definitive therapy alone or in conjunction with external beam radiotherapy. Several treatment guidelines exist, but the American Brachytherapy Society Guidelines provide a comprehensive overview of treatment indications, technical requirements, and current evidence for the most common disease sites.

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Therapy -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment was traditionally administered with naturally occurring radioactive elements that give off photons during the decay process. However, in the modern era, these are typically generated by machines called linear accelerators, which accelerate a stream of electrons toward a target, producing photons resulting from the atomic interactions occurring within that target. These photons are directed toward the patient through a mobile gantry, and motorized collimators are used to shape the radiation beam as it exits the gantry head.

retrieved_docs.title: Mohs Micrographic Surgery: AAD Guidelines on Superficial Radiation and Brachytherapy -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Skin cancer is the most common form of cancer and can generally be categorized into melanoma and nonmelanoma skin cancer, which includes squamous cell carcinoma and basal cell carcinoma. Nonmelanoma skin cancer types are generally less aggressive and have a lower risk of metastasis compared to melanoma, the most serious form of skin cancer. Melanoma originates in melanocytes, the pigment-producing cells of the skin, and is characterized by its ability to spread rapidly to other organs if not detected early. Although nonmelanoma skin cancer typically manifests as localized growths or sores that are typically treatable and have high survival rates, they can also be locally destructive and have the potential to metastasize, making early detection vital. Radiation therapy, including superficial radiation therapy and brachytherapy, is a modality that uses x-ray or radioactive energy to treat nonmelanoma skin cancer. The activity reviews the guidelines and uses of superficial radiation therapy and brachytherapy in treating nonmelanoma skin cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation-Induced Cardiac Toxicity -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Radiation therapy is an important component in the treatment of cancer. It may play a role as an adjuvant, neoadjuvant, palliative, or definitive therapy, with or without concurrent chemotherapy. Radiation is most commonly delivered as a local/regional treatment by an external beam consisting of photons, electrons, protons, or heavy particles but may also be delivered via brachytherapy (where a sealed radiation source is placed adjacent to the target) or systemically via unsealed sources. The side effects of radiation therapy are a function of the tissues included in the radiation field. Treatment of diseases within the thoracic region, such as Hodgkin lymphoma, lung, and breast cancer, carries the risk of radiation-induced cardiovascular toxicity (RICT). [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Physics -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Radiation can be toxic when exposed in large amounts, and it is thus important to measure. The process of radioactive decay is random, and it is, therefore, difficult to predict when a particular atom will start to decay. However, it is possible to quantify the number of isotopes in a group that decays over a period of time. This rate of decay is called radioactivity, and it is proportional to the number of atoms of a certain radioactive material over its half-life. The half-life of an isotope is the time it takes for 50% of the nuclide in a group to decay. This rate is measured in Becquerels (Bq) in international units or Curies (Ci), which is used in the United States. By emitting particles, the radioactivity of an element can generate radiation. Radiation is clinically measured by the absorbed dose, which is equal to the amount of energy deposited in a specific region. It is calculated as the mean energy absorbed divided by the mass of the target material. In the practice of radiation oncology, the absorbed dose is measured as "gray" (Gy), with doses expressed in fractions over a time period. [7] [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Brachytherapy -- Function -- Image Guided Brachytherapy and Treatment Planning Systems
retrieved_docs.content: With the advent of advanced computing technology, more precise dosimetry became possible with CT, MRI, or even ultrasound-based brachytherapy planning. These advances are most evident in treating breast, cervical, and prostate cancer. Computational methods have significantly changed brachytherapy planning as it allows for 3-dimensional target definition, dwell weight optimization, and generation of isodose calculation. Unlike the older systems, which used idealized geometries and heuristics, CT-based planning allows for the evaluation of dose distribution in real-time in the patient using the actual implant geometry. Despite these advances, several assumptions derived from previous systems, such as 1 cm uniform spacing, uniform loading, and implanting at the boundary of the tumor, persist.

retrieved_docs.title: Brachytherapy -- Function -- Dose Rate
retrieved_docs.content: Brachytherapy is typically described by the rate at which the dose is delivered. Three categories have been established to categorize brachytherapy. These include high-dose-rate (HDR) brachytherapy delivered at more than 12 Gy/hour, Pulsed dose rate (PDR) delivered at 10-30 minute intervals at a rate of 0.5-1.0 Gy/hour, Medium dose rate (MDR) offered at 2-12 Gy/hour, and low dose rate (LDR) brachytherapy delivered at 0.4-2.0 Gy/hour. The advantages and disadvantages of LDR and HDR systems are listed in the table below.

retrieved_docs.content: Which atoms and their tagged molecules can be used in diagnostics. Which radioactive atoms can be used in therapeutics Precautions necessary to protect the general public from radiation Precautions necessary to minimize the patient's radiation The type of shielding and other protective measures necessary in the handling of certain radioactive products The proper disposal of radioactive substances in the nuclear medicine department

retrieved_docs.title: Radiopharmaceuticals -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Radiotherapeutic agents use the radiation emitted from the nuclide to kill the target cells or serve palliative purposes. Radiation is toxic to tissues in the body: the brain, spinal cord, kidneys, and bone marrow are especially susceptible. Many radiopharmaceuticals are delivered systemically, and this means that ideally, the pharmaceuticals should selectively prefer the tumor tissue relative to normal healthy tissue. Many various specific radionuclides are in common use. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Localized Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Definitive treatment of localized disease now includes radiation therapy (external beam and/or brachytherapy radioactive seed placement), radical prostatectomy, and cryotherapy (usually reserved for radiation therapy failures). Radiation therapy tends to have much fewer adverse effects (about 50% less) compared to radical prostatectomy surgery, with very similar OS.

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Physics -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Beta particles subdivide into negative and positive emissions. A negative beta emission has a single negative charge, and it increases the number of protons by one and decreases the number of neutrons by one. A positive beta emission contains a single positive charge, and it decreases the number of protons by one and increases the number of neutrons by one. In both cases, the atomic mass stays the same, but it forms a different element. Unlike alpha particles, beta particles can travel far and penetrate through water and tissue with high energy. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 159
retrieved_docs.content: stereotactic radiosurgery (الجراحة الإشعاعية التجسيمية) is positioned as a specialized application within the overall framework of examination using radioactive isotopes. Together, these interrelated concepts illustrate the hierarchical and functional relationships that define nuclear medicine imaging, its diagnostic procedures, therapeutic applications, and site‑specific studies, all of which are essential for comprehensive patient evaluation and care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 4 -- Community 2696
retrieved_docs.content: CDISC SDTM Terminology incorporates the concept sexuální partneři and the concept intrakavitární radioterapie, and it defines a subset that includes the concept test cesty. The relationship subset_includes_concept describes how these individual concepts are organized within the CDISC SDTM Terminology, ensuring that specialized terms such as sexual partners (sexuální partneři) and intracavitary radiotherapy (intrakavitární radioterapie) are properly categorized and that test cesty is recognized as part of a specific subset of the terminology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1502
retrieved_docs.content: Radiotherapy is recognized as an approved therapeutic qualifier for the condition described in Arabic as بيلة سِّيسْتينِية, indicating that it may be used as a treatment option for this bladder-related disorder. Additionally, the use of radiotherapy can be further specified or qualified by the Arabic term قساح, suggesting that particular criteria or subcategories apply when radiotherapy is employed for this condition. This highlights that radiotherapy not only serves as an allowable intervention for بيلة سِّيسْتينِية but also that its application may be refined according to the qualifiers denoted by قساح.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2876
retrieved_docs.content: CDISC SDTM Terminology is a standardized clinical data terminology that incorporates a subset of concepts, as indicated by the relationship “subset_includes_concept.” Among the concepts included in this subset are the urogenitální systém (urogenital system) and تقويم نظم القلب (cardiac system calibration). In addition, the terminology encompasses areas such as sexuální partneři (sexual partners) and intrakavitární radioterapie (intracavitary radiotherapy), reflecting its broad coverage of both anatomical systems and specific clinical interventions. By defining these diverse entities within a unified framework, CDISC SDTM Terminology facilitates consistent data representation and interoperability across medical research and patient care contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1540
retrieved_docs.content: Intrakavitární radioterapie is a specialized form of radiation therapy that targets lesions within body cavities; the two terms are closely related and may be considered synonymous in clinical practice, reflecting the same therapeutic approach of delivering ionizing radiation to treat intracavitary disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 4 -- Community 2695
retrieved_docs.content: CDISC SDTM Terminology is a standardized clinical data terminology that incorporates a specific subset of medical concepts. Within this subset, the terminology includes the concept “test cesty,” the concept “urogenitální systém” (the urogenital system), the concept “exploratory laparotomy,” and the concept “intrakavitární radioterapie” (intracavitary radiotherapy). By encompassing these diverse concepts—ranging from procedural terms to anatomical systems—CDISC SDTM Terminology supports consistent representation and exchange of clinical trial information across studies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 874
retrieved_docs.content: Therapeutic radiology and nuclear medicine, transmission imaging, and الجراحة الإشعاعية التجسيمية (stereotactic radiosurgery) are all organized under the broader category الفحص باستخدام النظائر المشعة (examination using radioactive isotopes). Therapeutic radiology and nuclear medicine is also classified under radiation therapy, reflecting its dual role in both diagnostic nuclear imaging and therapeutic radiation applications. Intrakavitární radioterapie (intracavitary radiotherapy) is described as being related to, and possibly synonymous with, radiation therapy, indicating that it is essentially a form of radiation treatment delivered within a body cavity. This hierarchical structure highlights the interrelationship between diagnostic nuclear imaging modalities, therapeutic radiation techniques, and specialized procedures such as stereotactic radiosurgery and intracavitary radiotherapy, underscoring their combined relevance in patient diagnosis and treatment planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2773
retrieved_docs.content: Radioterapie is linked to two specific qualifiers. The qualifier labeled “can be qualified by.” is associated with the term قساح, indicating that radiotherapy can be qualified by this concept. Additionally, the qualifier labeled “Allowed qualifier” is connected to the term فَرْطُ ضَغْطِ الدَّم العيني, meaning that radiotherapy is an allowed qualifier for excess intra‑ocular blood pressure. Together, these relationships highlight that radiotherapy may be contextualized or qualified by قساح and is recognized as an appropriate qualifier when addressing فَرْطُ ضَغْطِ الدَّم العيني in clinical considerations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 4 -- Community 2746
retrieved_docs.content: Radiotherapy is described as being able to be qualified by specific descriptors, and the allowed qualifiers for this therapeutic modality include the Arabic term “قساح” as well as the condition “تضيُّق الصمام المترالي” (mitral valve stenosis). This indicates that the application or categorization of radiotherapy may be contextualized by these particular qualifiers.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 4 -- Community 2885
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier denotes the set of permissible descriptors that may be applied to radioterapie. Radioterapie can be qualified by the terms تمزق, التموت, قساح, and ورم عضلي أملس, all of which are recognized as allowed qualifiers. Consequently, when documenting or discussing radioterapie, clinicians may appropriately use تمزق, التموت, قساح, or ورم عضلي أملس to specify the particular context or characteristic of the treatment. This clarification of allowable qualifiers supports precise communication and accurate recording of radioterapie‑related information in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 62
retrieved_docs.content: and is mapped to Radiotherapy (finding), illustrating its role as a radiotherapy session within the broader maintenance treatment framework. Together, these relationships depict a hierarchical and cross‑linked structure of chemotherapy and radiotherapy concepts, specific treatment regimens, associated adverse effects, and related procedural and patient‑behavioral contexts, all of which are important for comprehensive documentation, coding, and patient‑centered care planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2827
retrieved_docs.content: CDISC SDTM Terminology is a standardized clinical data terminology that encompasses a variety of medical concepts, including the Bone Mineral Density Test, the concept of sexual partners, and intracavitary radiotherapy. By incorporating these diverse items—ranging from a diagnostic assessment of bone health to a descriptor of interpersonal sexual relationships and a specific form of localized radiation treatment—CDISC SDTM Terminology provides a comprehensive framework for consistent data representation across clinical studies and health information systems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2037
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is a term used in radiotherapy to denote specific clinical conditions that are considered permissible or relevant when evaluating a patient for treatment. The allowed qualifiers for radiotherapy include respiratory failure, insufficiency, and arrest in adults, as well as عدوى بفيروس معوي (infection with an enteric virus). Recognizing these qualifiers helps clinicians assess patient suitability for radiotherapy, anticipate potential complications, and tailor therapeutic plans accordingly.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 596
retrieved_docs.content: treatment to advanced surgical techniques, imaging studies, supportive therapies, and coding classifications—providing a comprehensive clinical, historical and economic context for documentation, analysis and health‑system planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 821
retrieved_docs.content: tumor targeting. It is simultaneously placed under the parent categories “الفحص باستخدام النظائر المشعة” and radiation therapy, illustrating that therapeutic radiology and nuclear medicine is both a specialized form of isotope‑based examination and a subfield of conventional radiation therapy. Additionally, the Arabic term “الجراحة الإشعاعية التجسيمية” (stereotactic radiosurgery) is associated with the same parent category “الفحص باستخدام النظائر المشعة,” reinforcing the concept that stereotactic radiosurgery is considered an advanced, image‑guided therapeutic procedure within the isotope‑based examination framework. Collectively, these relationships depict a cohesive network in which diagnostic procedures, imaging actions, and therapeutic modalities are interlinked through shared methodologies (radionuclide imaging), hierarchical classifications (parent‑child structures), and overarching domains such as isotope‑based examinations and radiation therapy, providing a comprehensive view

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2118
retrieved_docs.content: الجراحة الإشعاعية التجسيمية (stereotactic radiosurgery) is classified as a subcategory of الفحص باستخدام النظائر المشعة (radioisotope imaging). This hierarchical relationship indicates that the precise, radiation‑based therapeutic technique of stereotactic radiosurgery falls within the broader domain of radiological procedures that employ radioactive isotopes for imaging purposes. Recognizing that الجراحة الإشعاعية التجسيمية is conceptually linked to الفحص باستخدام النظائر المشعة underscores the shared radiological principles underlying both diagnostic imaging and targeted radiation therapy, aiding clinicians in integrating diagnostic and therapeutic strategies that rely on isotopic technologies.
